coredata.dc:title: Room temperature synthesized metal organic frameworks of Lawsonia inermis: Potential candidates for sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Sirajunnisa, P.
Sreelakshmi, S.
Prathapan, S.
Sailaja, G.S.
coredata.dc:description: A novel environmentally benign synthesis of Zn and Fe based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) of Lawsonia inermis leaf extract prepared in-house by a simple room temperature extraction technique is presented. BET isotherm and HRTEM analyses revealed mesoporous nature of the newly synthesized MOFs [6.4 nm (Zn-NLw), 3.6 nm (Fe-NLw)]. Their potential for host-guest interaction facilitating sensing applications was demonstrated by selective binding of histidine (His) and tryptophan (Trp) recognized from fluorescence. Addition of His and Trp, induced significant changes in the emission maxima of MOFs (at 470 nm) demonstrating their sensing capacity for these molecules. Host-guest interaction was further investigated by electrochemical studies evidenced as a function of variation in oxidation-reduction peaks. Cytocompatibility of the MOFs were assessed by MTT assay, impact on cellular architecture, degree of cellular uptake while their application in cell imaging was investigated by confocal microscopy analysis. The results suggest excellent biocompatibility of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw in vitro with significant level of uptake by cells. Fe-NLw exhibited time dependent subcellular localization with distinct luminescence characteristics at a short time period of 30 min as well as at 2 h. These results therefore validate the potential application of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw in biosensing and fluorescence based bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lawsonia inermis
Extraction
Metal organic frameworks
Fluorescence
Amino acid sensing
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Aqueous extraction of lawsone from lawsonia inermis
Room temperature synthesis of MOFs using extracted lawsone

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by University Grants Commission ( UGC, India ) and DBT-BIOCARE grant.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs and structural evaluation of isolated lawsone from Lawsonia inermis (a) leaves, (b) powder (c) UV–Vis spectrum, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e)1H NMR and GC-MS spectra.
(a) PXRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw.
Thermogravimetric profile of NLw, Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw.
Adsorption/desorption isotherms of (a) Zn-NLw and (b) Fe-NLw with corresponding pore size distribution via (c & d) BJH plots.
TEM images showing porous structures for (a–c) Zn-NLw and (d) Fe-NLw with different magnifications.
Emission spectra of increasing concentration of (a & c) His (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30 μM) and (b & d) Trp (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 μM) with (a,b) Zn-NLw and (c,d) Fe-NLw (0.5 mg mL−1) [λex: 400 nm].
CVs of the (a & b) Zn-NLw and (c & d) Fe-NLw modified electrodes in the presence of His and Trp in 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1with dotted lines represent the blank samples.

Cell viability results from MTT assay (A) after incubation of HOS with different concentrations of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw (20, 40, 60, 80 μg mL−1) (a) 24 h and (b)72 h. The untreated cells were taken as 100% viable cells. Error bars show standard deviations from three separate measurements. “Ns” indicates statistical nonsignificance (p > 0.05) whereas “*” (p < 0.05), “**” (p < 0.01), “***” (p < 0.001) and “****” (p < 0.0001) indicate statistical significance; (B) Cellular uptake by Prussian blue staining of (b & c) Fe-NLw treated cells and (a) control cells.
Cytoskeleton images of HOS cells incubated with Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw (20 μg mL−1) with control HOS cells. (Scale bar = 50 μm).

CLSM images of HOS cells incubated with 40 μg mL−1 of Fe-NLw for 30 min (top row) and 2 h (bottom row) incubations. (a & d) Bright-field, (b & e) excited with a 405 and 488 nm laser and (c & f) merged images of (a &b) and (d & e) respectively. Scale bar = 10 μm.

head.ce:title: Room temperature synthesized metal organic frameworks of Lawsonia inermis: Potential candidates for sensing and cellular imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Rubber Technology
Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel environmentally benign synthesis of Zn and Fe based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) of Lawsonia inermis leaf extract prepared in-house by a simple room temperature extraction technique is presented. BET isotherm and HRTEM analyses revealed mesoporous nature of the newly synthesized MOFs [6.4 nm (Zn-NLw), 3.6 nm (Fe-NLw)]. Their potential for host-guest interaction facilitating sensing applications was demonstrated by selective binding of histidine (His) and tryptophan (Trp) recognized from fluorescence. Addition of His and Trp, induced significant changes in the emission maxima of MOFs (at 470 nm) demonstrating their sensing capacity for these molecules. Host-guest interaction was further investigated by electrochemical studies evidenced as a function of variation in oxidation-reduction peaks. Cytocompatibility of the MOFs were assessed by MTT assay, impact on cellular architecture, degree of cellular uptake while their application in cell imaging was investigated by confocal microscopy analysis. The results suggest excellent biocompatibility of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw in vitro with significant level of uptake by cells. Fe-NLw exhibited time dependent subcellular localization with distinct luminescence characteristics at a short time period of 30 min as well as at 2 h. These results therefore validate the potential application of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw in biosensing and fluorescence based bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural products and their derivatives are promising alternatives in healthcare sector presenting wide range of applications especially in drug delivery and biosensing/imaging. Plant-based materials were not encouraged by pharmaceutical companies for several years, owing to the existence of a myth that the natural products are useful only as a restricted class of drugs [1]. As a result, major pharmaceutical companies focused synthetic compound libraries for drug discovery, while only limited efforts were directed towards examining the potential of natural products [1–3]. Nevertheless, introduction of natural raw materials for pharmaceutical applications has turned attractive option recently because of their several potential advantages over synthetic materials viz economical, readily available, non-toxic nature, susceptive to chemical modifications, biodegradable and biocompatible (most of the cases) [4–7]. Even though there exist certain challenges like small quantity synthesis, existence as mixtures owing to complex structure, variability with respect to location of plants as well as season, natural materials are indeed renewable resources ensuring continuous supply of raw materials [8].

Compared to conventional methods, development of nanomaterials from green precursors is an environmentally friendly approach offering several additional benefits including minimum side effect, abundance, renewability, sustainability etc [9]. Various plant parts, including the root, stem, leaf, fruit, flower, and seed are being explored as low-value resources that can be converted into functional materials with excellent biocompatibility. However, there is still a high demand for the natural materials derived from various plant parts for the specific biomedical applications such biosensors and bioimaging [9–12].

Lawsonia inermis Linn, also known as henna plant, contains carbohydrates, phenolic, flavanoids, saponins, proteins, alkaloids, terpenoids, quinones, coumarins, xanthones, fat, resin and tannins as phytochemical constituents. Lawsone (2-hydroxy-1, 4-naphthaquinone, Fig. 1 ) is a natural pigment present in the extract of dried powdered leaves of Lawsonia inermis with a variety of structural and functional applications in the field of synthetic chemistry [13–15] and has pharmacological relevance including anti-inflammatory and anticancer activities. Furthermore, lawsone is susceptible to metal complexation reactions due to its existence in three different forms: quinone, semiquinone and catechol; which make it feasible to bind with metals of different oxidation states. This binding ability of quinone form of lawsone is believed to play an important role in pharmaceutical and biochemistry with improved biological activities. Several methods have been adopted for the synthesis of lawsone which either begins with construction of a naphthoquinone system followed by modification or by starting from naphthalene derivatives [16].

Most of the methods developed so far for the synthesis of lawsone presents environmental impacts presenting the high need for extraction based techniques [16–19]. Conventional methods of extraction include maceration, soxhletation and reflux methods which requires abundant amount of solvent, provide lower yield and longer extraction time. As an alternative to these, this study presents an efficient, environmentally benign, and simple room temperature aqueous extraction technique by appropriate optimization of the conditions and parameters.

There is enormous demand for organic-inorganic hybrid materials in catalysis, drug delivery, adsorption, separations, non-linear optics, microelectronic, sensing/imaging [20,21], where the better properties provided by them compared to their individual counterparts are due to the inorganic part supporting the mechanical, thermal and structural stability while the flexibility and functionality are derived from organic molecules [22,23]. Among the hybrids, metal organic frameworks (MOFs), formed via self-assembly of metal and organic ligand are significant in the drug delivery systems, sensing and imaging especially due to their broad inner surface area and flexible architecture [24–26]. Inherent porosity, structural diversity, and adjustable physicochemical properties are important attributes of MOFs for these kinds of applications. They also make an excellent platform for the design of chemical sensors due to their distinctive structural tailoring and performance modulation. Multiple hydroxyl-group-based materials are frequently utilized for sensing, and MOFs serve this purpose well [27].

Fluorescence-based sensing has remarkable advantages over traditional detection methods including high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry and spectrophotometry, for early diagnosis and monitoring of diseases, due to its economic affordability, convenience, rapid response, high sensitivity, good repeatability, efficiency etc. This has generated a great deal of interest and several investigations in this regard are reported [28–30]. Luminescent MOFs have been extensively studied as sensing materials for metal ions, small organic molecules, drug molecules and nitroaromatic compounds. In addition, the aqueous stability of luminescent MOFs is crucial for studying their sensing performances. The combination of porosity and potential open sites within the MOFs confer novel sensing functionality in these materials. Moreover, Lewis acidic, Lewis basic and π-conjugated MOF frameworks can offer combinatorial interactions for sensing [29,31]. As fluorescent sensors, MOFs typically detect analytes via fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence shift. Fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence shift have been reported significantly less frequently than fluorescence quenching. So, it is very important to make MOF based fluorescent sensors that are more sensitive and easier to detect [32,33].

However, it is imperative to ensure the non-toxicity of the systems in order to be used in the biological domains and therefore selection of components during the material development should be in such a way that it is environmentally benign and biologically compatible [34–36]. Moreover, among various biological molecules, amino acids (AAs) are indispensable constituents of living systems, and their detection could be explored both in nutritional analysis and disease diagnosis [37–39].

In light of this, an environmentally benign synthetic process has been developed for Zn and Fe based MOFs from lawsone extracted in-house under mild synthetic conditions and is presented. The as-synthesized plant-based MOFs have appreciable pore size and excellent cytocompatibility and exhibited selective fluorescence detection and cellular imaging, and hence could be explored for sensing and bioimaging applications.

All the reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. The leaves of Lawsonia inermis were collected from the local garden located in Malappuram, Kerala, India. Sodium bicarbonate, Zinc (II) nitrate hexahydrate, sodium acetate trihydrate and diethyl ether were procured from Merck, India. Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate was purchased from LobaChemie, India, while anhydrous methanol, l-histidine and l-tryptophan were obtained from Spectrochem, India. Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm, Direct-Q 3, 5, 8 UV system) was used for all the experiments. TLC plates precoated with silica gel (GF254) were supplied by Merck, Germany to monitor the extracted lawsone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aqueous extraction of lawsone from lawsonia inermis
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh leaves of henna were washed with water to remove dirt and other impurities followed by shade drying for several days at room temperature. Dried leaves were pulverized to reduce particle size for effective extraction and stored in an airtight container. Dry powdered henna (100 g) in 5% sodium bicarbonate solution (500 mL) was extracted under mechanical stirring (600 rpm) at room temperature for an hour. The reddish orange suspension obtained was then heated at 70 °C for another hour to split the glycosidic bond and centrifuged (7000 rpm) after cooling. Lawsone contained in the extract turned the solution into orange and pH of the extract was adjusted to neutral using 5% HCl and dried. It was then purified using ethyl acetate-ethanol mixture (2:1), labelled as NLw, and used for MOF synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Room temperature synthesis of MOFs using extracted lawsone
ce:section.ce:para: MOFs were synthesized at room temperature using the Lawsonia inermis extracted lawsone (NLw) in presence of Zn(II) and Fe(III) ions. Our recently reported procedure was followed for the MOF synthesis [40]. Briefly, NLw solution in methanol was mixed with corresponding metal ion solutions at room temperature for 24 h before being treated with saturated sodium acetate trihydrate. After sedimentation of orange red (Zn) and brownish black (Fe) precipitates, the supernatant solutions were decanted, and the as-synthesized MOFs were washed with diethyl ether for 3–4 times. The final products, Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw, were dried in an oven at 50 °C for 18 h. The reaction was repeated at 60 °C following the same protocol to examine the effect of temperature, and the products were designated as NZn60 and NFe60.

Fluorescence characteristics of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw MOFs in aqueous suspension were examined at room temperature within the wavelength range 400–700 nm upon excitation at 400 nm. In order to explore the sensing capacity of the MOFs for amino acids, the as-synthesized MOFs (0.5 mg mL−1) were dispersed in Milli-Q water containing different amino acids (0.1 M) including l-Aspartic acid, l-glutamic acid, glycine, l-lysine, l-histidine, l-arginine, l-valine, l-cysteine, l-tryptophan and l-glutamine. The absorbance spectra analyzed in the range 200–600 nm exhibit selectivity of both Zn and Fe MOFs towards His and Trp. Further, the emission spectra of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw were recorded by gradual addition of His (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30 μM) and Trp (0.0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 μM). Stable suspensions could be stored after preparation for prolonged periods of time without affecting their fluorescent emission. In addition, anti-interference experiments were performed by sequentially adding solutions of other AAs along with His/Trp (0.1 M) to a water suspension of MOF (0.5 mg mL−1) and observed variations in the fluorescence intensity. To obtain accurate data, each experiment was repeated three times [37].

Electrochemical performance of the as-synthesized MOFs with respect to different concentrations of His and Trp addition was analyzed further to evaluate the sensing behavior following three-electrode method via cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 0.1 M KCl electrolyte at a constant scan rate (50 mVs−1). The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) drop coated with Zn-NLw/Fe-NLw- His/Trp dispersion (10 μL) was allowed to evaporate at room temperature after adding Nafion as binder, which acts as the working electrode. Control experiments were carried out using GCE modified with Zn-NLw/Fe-NLw MOFs. The reference electrode is made of Ag/AgCl, and platinum wire is employed as the counter electrode. For the Zn and Fe systems, the voltage varied between −1.5 V and +1.5 and −1 to +1 respectively.

Structural, morphological, optical and thermal stability of as-synthesized MOFs were investigated using various analytical techniques. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Bruker AXS D8 with Cu Kα radiation) was used to demonstrate the formation of MOFs. The functionalities and metal coordination of extract and MOFs were determined using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet Avatar 370) run by the KBr method from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 taken in a transmission mode. Proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR, Jeol JNM ECZ400 S/L1, 400 MHz, D2O) and Mass spectrometry (Agilent Gas Chromatogram/Mass Spectrometry, GC-MS) were used to further evaluate the structure of isolated lawsone. Ultrastructures of samples were identified from Jeol/JEM 2100 HR-TEM microscope, with accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The temperature dependence of the samples was studied via Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TA Instruments) with a rate of 20 °C min−1 in the range 30–800 °C (N2 atmosphere). Porosity and surface area calculations were made with BELSORP-Max adsorption apparatus at 77K. ELICO SL 210 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (UV-Vis) was used to measure absorbance of MOFs and fluorescence spectra (Agilent Technologies) were examined in the 400–800 nm range with excitation wavelength of 400 nm using slit width of 3 nm. Cyclic voltammetry data were collected using Electrochemical workstation, CHI660E at room temperature.

Human osteosarcoma cells (HOS) obtained from NCCS, Pune, India, were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM; Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (Gibco, USA) and 1% antibiotic solution (10,000 units per mL of penicillin and 10,000 mg mL−1 of streptomycin) (Sigma Aldrich, India). After attaining 90% confluency, the cells were trypsinized using trypsin/EDTA (0.25% trypsin/0.02% EDTA) (Sigma Aldrich, India), and the cell suspension was used to evaluate the cytocompatibility of MOF samples.

Cytocompatibility of the MOF samples was evaluated by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide [MTT] (Sigma Aldrich, India) assay performed in HOS cells. The absorbance of MTT at 570 nm depends on the degree of activation of cells, which is calculated from the ratio of the absorbance of treated cells to that of control cells. The cells (5 × 103 cells/well) were cultured in 96 well plates at standard conditions (37 °C and 5% CO2) for 24 h to ensure cell adherence. The adhered cells were then incubated with syringe-filtered sterile samples (NLw, Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw) of varying concentrations ranging from 20 to 80 μg mL−1 (n = 4) for 24 and 72 h under the same culture conditions. At the pre-defined time points, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1 in phosphate buffered saline, PBS) was added and incubated for 3 h. The formazan crystals were solubilized by dimethyl sulfoxide (100 μL) (Merck, India). After 20 min of incubation at room temperature, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Spark Multimode Microplate Reader, Tecan, Switzerland). The cells maintained the same way without any treatment were used as the control, while the culture medium without cells served as the blank.

The HOS (5 × 103 cells/well) were seeded on surface-treated coverslips in a 24 well plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. After attaining their normal adhered morphology, the samples (Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw) with 20 μg mL−1 concentration were incubated with the cells for another 24 h. After removing the culture medium, the cells were fixed with 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde (Sigma Aldrich, India) solution for 30 min and subsequently permeabilized with 50% ice-cold methanol for 10 min. After blocking with 2% bovine serum albumin (BSA, HiMedia, India), Alexa Fluor-488 phalloidin (Thermo Fisher Scientific, India) primary antibody prepared in BSA (1:100 dilution) was added for staining F-actin cytoskeleton (100 μL, 30 min) followed by counterstaining of the nuclei with DAPI (4’,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) (3 nM in PBS, 30 min). A phase-contrast inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon Eclipse Ti2 Series, India) was used to capture the fluorescent images.

The uptake of the Fe-NLw by the cells was evaluated by Prussian blue staining after culturing HOS (5 × 103 cells/well) with the sample at the concentration of 20 μg mL−1 in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. Following the incubation and washing with PBS, the cells were treated with 4% paraformaldehyde solution for 30 min, washed thrice with PBS to remove excess fixative and incubated again with a solution of 10% potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) (Merck, India) in HCl for another 10 min. The excess potassium ferrocyanide was removed, and the cells were repeatedly washed with sterile Milli-Q water and observed under phase-contrast inverted microscope (Nikon Eclipse Ti2 Series, India) [40].

The MOFs were investigated for imaging property in HOS cells by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The cells (5 × 103 cells/well) were incubated with MOFs (40 μg mL−1 in complete DMEM) for 30 min and 2 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. After fixation for 15 min and subsequent washing with PBS, the specimens were observed using confocal microscope (Nikon, oil immersion lens 60x magnification) with excitation of 405 and 488 nm laser.

The viability was further evaluated after treating the cells with MOFs by fluorescein diacetate (FDA) and propidium iodide (PI). Briefly, HOS cells seeded in a 24 well plate with a cell density of 5 × 103 cells/well were incubated with 20 μg mL−1 concentrations of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw for 24 h at standard conditions. After removing the medium, the cells were given two PBS washes, and then 300 μL FDA (5 mg mL−1 stock in acetone) with 1:500 dilution in an incomplete medium (DMEM) was added in the dark and left for 10 min. Following the addition of 2 μL of PI with 1:100 dilution in an incomplete medium, the cells were incubated for an additional 2 min and observed using a Nikon-Ti2 microscope.

In Lawsonia inermis leaves, lawsone can be found as glycosidic precursors that have been cleaved by heating to 70 °C before being isolated. TLC was used to monitor the extraction and isolation process, while UV–Vis, FTIR, 1H NMR, and GC-MS analyses were used to validate the results (Fig. 2 ).

UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2c) recorded in methanol depicted the commonly observed n to π* and π to π* transitions of lawsone at 274, 340 and 440 nm due to the presence of benzene and quinone chromophores. FTIR (Fig. 2d) revealed the existence of characteristic bands associated with aromatic CC stretching in the region 1480-1330 cm−1. The absorption band at 3460 cm−1 corresponding to the hydroxyl stretching vibration and α, β-unsaturated carbonyl band appeared at 1720 and 1660 cm−1 [41]. Resonance peaks at 5.62 ppm, 7.48–7.75 ppm and 8.32 ppm associated with the phenolic, aromatic and alkenyl protons in 1H NMR (Fig. 2e) recorded in D2O could be used to determine the presence of lawsone. Fragmentation patterns of lawsone (m/z 105, 146, 77) along with its molecular ion peak (m/z 174) was identified from GC-MS (Fig. 2f), which further confirmed the structure of isolated lawsone [42].

Formation of lawsone derived MOFs was confirmed from PXRD analysis which elucidated formation of highly crystalline Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw (Fig. 3 a). Strong distinct peaks at 8.6° and 8.5° of the as-prepared samples present in the XRD patterns indicated formation of MOF. Sharp peaks in both patterns corroborate the crystalline structure while the average crystallite size of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw MOFs was estimated as 31.6 and 30 nm respectively as per Debye-Scherrer equation. It is to be noted that a decrease in crystallinity was observed with increasing temperature to 60 °C for Zn and Fe systems, denoted as NZn60 and NFe60 (Fig. S1), which could be associated with structural destruction. These findings are consistent with those obtained from previous studies [40,43].

FTIR data, as detailed in Fig. 3b, further revealed the formation of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw MOFs. Broad absorption bands appearing at 3600-3400 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibrations of adsorbed H2O molecules together with the hydroxyl peaks in the ligand molecule. In addition, both materials exhibit peaks corresponding to aromatic = C–H, CO and C–O stretch at 3055 cm−1, 1685-1670 cm−1 and 1279 cm−1 respectively. The aromatic and alkenyl C–H bending vibrations in the ligand moiety show absorption bands from 860 to 725 cm−1. Furthermore, the stretching vibration of CO has obvious red shift in both Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw as compared with that of extracted lawsone NLw at 1633 cm−1, showing the coordination of metal ions (Zn2+/Fe3+) with the enolic oxygen. Absorption bands in the regions of 1550 cm−1 and 1430-1400 cm−1 provided evidence for structural aromaticity [44,45]. Additionally, absence of the peaks of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw at 490 and 660 cm−1, which may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of Zn–O and Fe–O, respectively, in the spectrum of NLw indicates that the ligand has been successfully coordinated to the metal centers.

To examine the thermal stability of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw, thermogravimetric analysis was carried out between 25 and 700 °C at N2 atmosphere and compared with that of NLw (Fig. 4 ). The as-synthesized MOFs exhibited distinct degradation steps starting with weight loss in the range 25–100 °C corresponding to the removal of adsorbed water molecules. In the case of Zn-NLw, a plateau was observed in the range of 25–110 °C followed by a continuous loss of 7.5% up to 350 °C, which could be ascribed to the removal of guest molecules entrapped in the pores and adsorbed on the surface [46]. There is an extensive mass loss of 27% up to 600 °C corresponding to the secondary mass loss event, where the degradation of MOF begins. Degradation profile of Fe-NLw commences with the solvent evaporation in the range 25–80 °C, followed by a gradual loss of 3% up to a temperature of 250 °C and then there is a final degradation appears up to 700 °C. In both TGA profiles, the plateau region indicates that the sample mass is roughly constant which confirms the stability of solvent free MOFs [40,47]. Weight loss of the NLw moiety occurs between 105 and 470 °C, and the thermograms of the MOF could not exhibit this, showing that there is no free NLw in the framework structure. As it can be seen from the profiles, the masses of NLw, Zn-NLw, and Fe-NLw each retained about 34%, 59% and 55% of their initial mass after heating to temperatures around 700 °C, further demonstrating their thermal stability. In addition, disintegration of the framework structure occurred for NZn60 and NFe60, as a result of increase in the reaction temperature to 60 °C, as indicated in Fig. S2 which is supported by Fig. S1 [47,48].

Absorption spectra of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw (Fig. S3) revealed their sensing capabilities with some AAs, exhibiting some changes in the absorption maxima for His and Trp addition while other AAs showed no discernible change. Fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 7 ) recorded by gradual addition different concentrations of His and Trp reveal the change in emission intensities with slight shift in the emission maxima (λem: 470 nm), which indicates their sensing capability towards His and Trp.

The π-π stacking interaction between the aromatic moieties of the ligand lawsone and the imidazole/indole groups of His/Trp serve as the basis for the mechanism underlying the amino acid sensing performance of the MOFs. Hydrogen bond also has a significant effect on the fluorescence mechanism. In the case of Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw, addition of His resulted in a concentration-dependent enhancement of fluorescence, which may be attributed to the significantly enhanced π-π stacking interaction that facilitates electron transfer reactions (Fig. 7a and c). The fluorescence enhancement is also aided by the hydrogen bonding between His and the ligand. However, Zn-NLw initially exhibits a fluorescence quenching effect upon the addition of Trp, whereas Fe-NLw exhibits an increase in intensity up to 5 μM before exhibiting a quenching effect (10 μM) (Fig. 7b and d). This phenomenon could be explained via strong hydrogen bonds between the oxygen atoms of the ligand lawsone and the Trp molecules, which are expected to be present in the pores of MOF. The results indicate that, unlike His and Trp, other AAs had no discernible effect on the emission spectrum. It could be viewed that His and Trp interact more strongly than any other AAs, resulting in a change in the fluorescence intensities in presence of these AAs [52]. Calibration curve of relative emission intensity (I0/I) against quencher concentration (Stern-Volmer plot), which provided a linear plot with a correlation coefficient of 0.94, can be used to explain the observed fluorescence quenching. Altogether, significant changes in position and intensity can be attributed to the stronger hydrogen bonding interaction as well as the π-π stacking interaction when comparing other AAs with His and Trp [37,52].

The detection limits were calculated with formula 3σ/k (σ = standard error; k = slope) are 4.35 (His) and 0.80 μM (Trp) for Zn-NLw, while those for Fe-NLw are 3.84 (His) and 0.16 μM (His), indicating comparatively low detection limits for both systems. Several AAs usually coexist in biological systems. The ability to recognize a target analyte in the presence of other competing species is therefore of utmost importance. Anti-interference studies were carried out to demonstrate this by gradually adding aqueous solutions of other AAs following His/Trp (Fig. S4). It can be seen from Figs. S4a and b, introduction of other AAs (0.5 mL, 0.1 M) experienced no discernible effect on the fluorescence intensities of Zn-NLw. The intensity increased significantly with the addition of His while decreased with the addition of Trp. The addition of His and Trp solutions increased the intensity in the case of Fe-NLw (Figs. S4c and d), whereas the addition of other competing AAs displayed no appreciable impact. The observation implied that His and Trp can be detected by Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw in the presence of other AAs [31,37].

To support the results obtained from the fluorescence studies, CV responses were also monitored (Fig. 8 ). As seen in Fig. 8a and b, the gradual addition of His caused the reduction peak current at 0.1 V to drop from 3 μA, demonstrating the presence of electron transfer from Zn-NLw to the analyte molecule. Likewise, Trp addition resulted in a decrease in the reduction peak with simultaneous increase of oxidation peak current at lower potential (−0.1 V) with maximum current at highest concentration (10 μM). Moreover, interesting changes in the oxidation and reduction peaks are seen for Fe-NLw (Fig. 8c and d) with respect to His/Trp additions. The changes attribute to the π-π interaction and other electron transfer reactions between MOFs and the analyte molecules (His/Trp), can be correlated with the fluorescence studies [53].

A MOF must have certain characteristics in order to function as a sensor. It must exhibit some change in response to an interaction with an analyte and this change must be analyte specific, and detectable. As-prepared MOFs meet these requirements and can thus function as sensors.

Cell imaging property of the MOFs investigated using CLSM demonstrated that Fe-NLw MOF could successfully label HOS cells as evident from the blue-green fluorescence (Fig. 11 b and e). As shown in Fig. 11b, significant blue fluorescence was observed in nuclear region at 30 min suggesting that Fe-NLw could pass through the membrane and enter the nucleus. At 2 h, a green fluorescence was observed to be distributed in the cytoplasmic region while fluorescence at the nuclear region dampened (Fig. 11e) [53]. This shift could be attributed to the interaction of Fe-NLw with AAs as mentioned before. These results indicate that Fe-NLw MOF can be effectively delivered to both cytoplasm and nucleus of cells, and are revealed to display a time dependent change in subcellular localization of the fluorescence. This property of Fe-NLw can be explored for their application in live cell imaging [54–56].

From all these evaluations, it can be concluded that both Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw with intrinsic fluorescent behavior do not appear to be toxic at the given dosage. Though both samples exhibited >80% cell viability, at the chosen concentration and time window Fe-NLw presents noticeable fluorescence for imaging applications. On the basis of the above results, it can be concluded that Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw should be safe for use in biomolecule sensing as well as cellular imaging.

In summary, we have accomplished a novel environmentally benign synthesis of Zn and Fe based MOFs from the Lawsonia inermis leaf extract prepared in-house at mild synthetic conditions with appreciable pore size suitable for the host-guest interaction. As-synthesized Zn-NLw and Fe-NLw MOFs with inherent fluorescent behavior demonstrated selective binding towards His and Trp, validated via fluorescence and electrochemical studies and indicated their potential to be used for quantitative estimation of His/Trp. Fe-NLw owing to its excellent cytocompatibility and significant internalization by HOS cells and fluorescent labelling properties of the cells complemented with time dependent subcellular localization of the fluorescence, could be explored for sensing and cellular imaging applications.

P. Sirajunnisa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. S. Sreelakshmi: Methodology. S. Prathapan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. G. S. Sailaja: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
This work was financially supported by University Grants Commission (UGC, India) and DBT-BIOCARE grant.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are greatly thankful to the Department of Biotechnology, CUSAT for providing the cell culture facilities. The authors are also grateful to Sophisticated Test and Instrumentation Centre (STIC), CUSAT for extending characterization facilities and Department of Physics, CUSAT for fluorescence measurement.
